name: EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA
definition: PULMONARY INFILTRATION WITH BLOOD / TISSUE EOSINOPHILIA (PIE) eosinophilic vasculitis TRANSIENT PULMONARY EOSINOPHILIA = LöFFLER SYNDROME benign disorder of unknown etiology characterized by local areas of transient parenchymal rare disorder characterized by overproduction of eosinophils (= primary

demographics.Path: interstitial + alveolar edema containing a large number of eosinophils
demographics.Distribution: uni- or bilateral, nonsegmental distribution, predominantly in lung periphery
demographics.Etiology: idiopathic (no evidence of infection / exposure to potential antigens) with abrupt
demographics.Histo: eosinophilic infiltrates + pulmonary edema ← release of eosinophilic granules

dominant_finding: patchy consolidation / nodules with peripheral and upper lobe predominance
discriminating_features: nonspecific peripheral airspace consolidation with ground-glass-opacity
nonspecific focal mass / lobular consolidation with atelectasis
CHARACTERISTIC photographic negative of pulmonary edema (in 25-34%)
nonspecific focal / diffuse interstitial or alveolar nonlobar opacities

general: nonspecific peripheral airspace consolidation with ground-glass-opacity
bronchiectasis ± mucoid impaction in central upper lungs
nonspecific focal mass / lobular consolidation with atelectasis
subpleural consolidation in a lobular distribution
CXR: peripheral patchy migratory / fleeting (= transient and shifting) noncavitary pleural-based
NO associated lymphadenopathy, NO pleural effusion
HRCT: peripheral ground-glass and/or airspace consolidation with upper and middle lobe
solitary / multiple pulmonary nodular opacities with surrounding ground-glass halo (rare)
diffuse bilateral reticular opacities mimicking pulmonary interstitial edema
bilateral patchy areas of ground-glass opacities
crazy-paving pattern (= ground-glass attenuation with intralobular reticular opacities)
frequently multifocal bilateral > unilateral nonsegmental with predilection for upper lung
CHARACTERISTIC photographic negative of pulmonary edema (in 25-34%)
unchanged for many days to weeks (DDx to Löffler syndrome)
fast regression of infiltrates under steroids
CT: patchy consolidation / nodules with peripheral and upper lobe predominance
subpleural reticulations (less common early, but frequent in later stages of disease)
linear bandlike opacities parallel to pleural surface (> 2 months after onset of symptoms)
focal / diffuse areas of ground-glass opacities in a patchy + peripheral distribution
Lungs (involved in 40%): congestive heart failure with pulmonary edema
nonspecific focal / diffuse interstitial or alveolar nonlobar opacities
